sources.title: Kaldi - Wikipedia
sources.author: Wikipedia contributors
sources.notes: Documents that the Kaldi legend was first written down in 1671 by Antoine Faustus Nairon; treats it as folklore rather than documented history.

sources.title: Yemen vs. Ethiopia | Coffee's True Origin Debate
sources.author: Sagebrush Coffee Editorial
sources.notes: Discusses the distinction between Ethiopia as the plant's geographic origin and Yemen as the site of the first documented beverage consumption by Sufi monks in the mid-15th century.

sources.title: History of coffee - Wikipedia
sources.author: Wikipedia contributors
sources.notes: Comprehensive reference article covering the spread from Yemen to Mecca (1414), Ottoman Empire (1554-55), Europe (1645 Venice, 1652 London), and the Americas (1720 Martinique, 1727 Brazil). Notes the 2020 Scalabrin genetic study. Used as cross-check for multiple date claims. Not sole source for any figure.

sources.title: Lloyd's Coffee House - Wikipedia; Jonathan's Coffee-House - Wikipedia
sources.author: Wikipedia contributors
sources.notes: Documents Lloyd's Coffee House (opened 1686 by Edward Lloyd) as origin of Lloyd's of London insurance market. Jonathan's Coffee-House Wikipedia article documents its role as origin of the London Stock Exchange (c.1680, formally renamed 1773). Cross-checked with history.co.uk and rgadvantage.com.

sources.title: How Coffee Fueled Revolutions—and Revolutionary Ideas
sources.author: HISTORY editorial staff
sources.notes: Authoritative popular-history source on coffeehouses as intellectual and political hubs. Documents penny universities concept, King Charles II's failed 11-day ban (1675), Café Procope's Enlightenment role, Ottoman bans under Murad IV, Boston's Green Dragon Tavern. Cross-checked with History.co.uk and retrospectjournal.com.

sources.title: World coffee production in 2024/25 is estimated at 176.2 million bags
sources.author: Tridge Research Team
sources.notes: Provides production data for 2024/25 crop year: global total 176.2 million 60-kg bags; Brazil 54.78M bags (31%), Vietnam 29.1M bags (16.5%), Colombia 12.4M bags (7%). Arabica 99.9M bags, Robusta 76.4M bags. Cross-checked with USDA FAS circular.

sources.title: Convergent evolution of caffeine in plants by co-option of exapted ancestral enzymes
sources.publisher: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences (PNAS)
sources.author: Huang et al.
sources.notes: Peer-reviewed academic study demonstrating that caffeine evolved independently at least five times in flowering plants (coffee, tea, cacao, guarana, citrus) via different biosynthetic pathways. Cross-checked with rarebird.coffee summary and ResearchGate abstract.

sources.title: No, coffee is not the second-most traded commodity after oil
sources.author: PolitiFact staff
sources.notes: Fact-check debunking the 'second most traded commodity' claim made by Starbucks to the US Senate. Cites MIT Observatory of Economic Complexity (green coffee ranked ~114th), and notes multiple commodities — wheat, soybeans, aluminum, copper, iron ore — have substantially larger global markets. Cross-checked with perfectdailygrind.com and SCA statement.

sources.title: Coffee Consumption Statistics 2026 - Global & U.S. Data
sources.author: Verena Street Research
sources.notes: Aggregates multiple industry sources. Key figures used: 2.25 billion cups/day (attributed to Specialty Coffee Association, Q3 2025); Luxembourg 12.5 kg/capita/year (rank 1); Finland 9.6 kg (rank 2, 4.1 cups/day); US 66% of adults drink daily (highest in 20 years); 3.1 average cups/drinker/day; 68% brew at home. Cross-checked with coffeedasher.com and economyinsights.com.

sources.title: USDA forecasts world coffee production for 2025/26 at an all-time high of 178.8 million bags (+2%), consumption to reach a record 173.9 million (+1.3%)
sources.author: Comunicaffe editorial staff
sources.notes: Reports on USDA Foreign Agricultural Service projections: production 178.8M bags (record), consumption 173.9M bags (record) for 2025/26. Cross-checked with USDA FAS direct publication and ecofinagency.com report.

sources.title: The biology of coffee, one of the world's most popular drinks
sources.author: Academic contributor (The Conversation)
sources.notes: Explains caffeine's mechanism as adenosine receptor blocker; covers genetic variation in caffeine sensitivity; discusses coffee's antioxidant properties and associations with reduced risk of Parkinson's, Type 2 diabetes, and certain cancers. Source for bitterness composition figure (caffeine = 10-15% of perceived bitterness) cross-checked with coffeechemistry.com.

sources.title: Coffee and Colonialism / History of Labor on Coffee Plantations
sources.author: Coffee Annan editorial
sources.notes: Documents the colonial history of coffee cultivation, including the 1727 Palheta/Brazil smuggling story, the role of enslaved African labor (1.7 million enslaved Africans transported to Brazil 1700-1800), and Haiti supplying half the world's coffee by 1788. Cross-checked with Wikipedia 'Brazilian coffee cycle' article and perfectdailygrind.com slavery article.

sources.title: Ottoman coffeehouse - Wikipedia
sources.author: Wikipedia contributors
sources.notes: Documents Ottoman coffeehouse social functions, 1554-55 Istanbul founding dates, range of clientele (merchants, madrasa students, janissaries), and role in political discourse. Cross-checked with lovekoffi.com and helvaciali.ca.

sources.title: The Long History of the Espresso Machine
sources.author: Smithsonian staff
sources.notes: Documents Angelo Moriondo's 1884 Turin patent as the first espresso machine; Luigi Bezzera's 1901 improvements; Desiderio Pavoni's commercialization from 1903. Cross-checked with Wikipedia Angelo Moriondo article and coffeeness.de. For instant coffee: Satori Kato's 1903 US patent cross-checked with Smithsonian Lemelson Center (invention.si.edu) and Satori Kato Wikipedia article.

sources.title: Third-wave coffee - Wikipedia
sources.author: Wikipedia contributors
sources.notes: Documents Trish Rothgeb coining 'third-wave coffee' in 2002 in The Flamekeeper (Roasters Guild newsletter). Lists pioneer companies: Intelligentsia (1995), Stumptown (1999), Counter Culture (1995). Notes Peet's Coffee (1966) and Starbucks (1971) as second-wave markers. Cross-checked with fourscorecoffee.com and cafealtura.com.

sources.title: Arabica vs Robusta: Complete Coffee Bean Guide 2025
sources.author: Espresso Barista editorial staff
sources.notes: Key facts used: Arabica caffeine ~1.5%, Robusta ~2.7%; Arabica altitude requirements 610-1830m; Robusta more disease-resistant; Arabica market share ~70% (historical average). Cross-checked with USITC report on Robusta trends, USDA data (Tridge), and sfbaycoffee.com.

sources.title: Coffee Market Size, Share, Trends & Forecast 2025-2033
sources.author: Renub Research analysts
sources.notes: Market research report estimating global coffee market at over $461 billion in 2024. Used only for market value figure; cross-checked against Statista and everydaypeoplecoffeeandtea.com for consistency.
